name: A/B Testing
def: A controlled online experiment that splits users between variants and measures the causal effect of a change on a chosen metric.
problem: Opinions and offline metrics do not reliably predict how a change affects real user behaviour and business outcomes.
mechanism: Randomly assign users to control (A) and treatment (B) variants behind a feature flag, hold everything else constant, and compare a predefined success metric with statistical tests until significance is reached. Randomisation gives a causal, not merely correlational, conclusion.
benefits: Limits risk by exposing changes gradually
limits: Peeking / multiple comparisons inflate false positives
Short-term metrics can hide long-term effects
failures: Optimising a proxy metric that diverges from real value
whenNot: Changes that cannot be ethically/technically split
Decisions needing a single consistent experience
myths: "Statistical significance = meaningful impact" — effect size and long-term effects matter too
